nativeRange.habitat: Like other Bucephalandra, the parent stock is endemic to the island of Borneo, where wild populations grow as rheophytes on rocks and submerged wood along the banks and beds of fast-flowing rainforest streams; aquarium-trade 'Black Pearl' plants are typically nursery-propagated from this lineage rather than wild-collected

decorPreferences: lava rock, driftwood, or other porous hardscape to attach the rhizome to using super glue gel, sewing thread, or fishing line
moderate flow across the rhizome to mimic rheophyte stream conditions
shaded or moderately lit areas; commonly placed in the foreground or midground of nano and mid-size tanks
stable, established tank — generally advised not to introduce into newly cycled systems

goodWith: shrimp (Neocaridina and Caridina) which graze biofilm without harming the plant
other low-light epiphytes such as Anubias, Java fern and mosses
avoidWith: large herbivorous or rhizome-disturbing fish such as goldfish, silver dollars and large plecos
vigorous diggers (large cichlids) that may dislodge attached rhizomes
newly set up tanks with unstable parameters

summary: 'Black Pearl' is a small, slow-growing Bucephalandra cultivar prized for its dark, almost black-green oblong leaves with an iridescent speckled sheen. Care is the same as the genus: attach the rhizome to rock or wood, keep in low-to-medium light, and never bury in substrate.
careNotes: 'Black Pearl' is a trade-named hobby cultivar of Bucephalandra (genus entry fw-plant-016), distinguished by its small, oblong dark-green to nearly black leaves with a raised central vein and a fine, iridescent white-speckled sheen visible under good lighting. Aquarium Co-Op describes it as a rare nano-suitable Buce with 'dark-colored leaves with a deep green to almost red color' that thrives under low light and is typically farm-raised rather than wild-collected; Buce Plant describes it as having 'dark, moody tones covered in tiny, reflective white dots that shimmer'. Husbandry is identical to the broader genus: the creeping rhizome is attached to lava rock, driftwood, or other porous hardscape with super glue gel, sewing thread, or fishing line and left exposed to the water column; burying the rhizome is generally reported to cause rot. Both reviewed sources rate the plant as easy, slow-growing, and tolerant of low to medium light; CO2 is generally considered optional but tends to improve growth and coloration. Reported water parameters cluster around pH 6.0-7.5 (Buce Plant) and the genus-level range of roughly 22-28 °C; gH around 3-15 °dGH is tolerated. Adult size is on the smaller end of the genus — comparable to other compact Buces — with mature clumps commonly staying in the 5-10 cm height range. Like other Bucephalandra, 'Black Pearl' is prone to a temporary melt of pre-existing leaves when first transitioned from emersed nursery growth to a submerged tank, or after sudden swings in temperature, pH or TDS; as long as the rhizome stays firm and pale green/white inside, new submersed-form leaves are generally reported to emerge from the rhizome over the following weeks. Best introduced into stable, established tanks. Care is widely summarized as 'similar to Anubias'.
breedingNotes: Propagation is by simple rhizome division: with sharp, clean scissors cut the rhizome at a natural bend or constriction so each division retains several leaves and visible roots, then reattach the new piece to a separate piece of hardscape. Sexual reproduction via the genus' characteristic spathe/spadix inflorescence is occasionally observed in emersed and paludarium setups but is not a practical hobby propagation method.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Primary source. Describes 'Black Pearl' as a rare, farm-raised nano Bucephalandra cultivar with dark green-to-almost-red small rounded leaves, low-light tolerance, slow growth via rhizome, and care similar to Anubias (rhizome attached to hardscape, never buried). Page does not provide explicit temperature/pH/GH ranges.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked cultivar identity (dark oblong leaves with raised central vein and iridescent white-speckled appearance), pH 6.0-7.5, low-to-medium lighting, CO2 not necessary but recommended, slow growth, propagation by rhizome division with sharp scissors, epiphytic mounting on driftwood or rock, easy care, and warning that burying the rhizome causes rot/melt.

additional.name: AquaMate genus entry (Bucephalandra, fw-plant-016)
additional.notes: Husbandry shared with the genus per playbook note. Genus-level parameter ranges (temperature 22-28 °C, pH 6.0-7.5, gH 3-15 °dGH, low-light, optional CO2, slow growth, rhizome attached to hardscape) are applied here because the two cultivar-specific sources confirm 'same care as Anubias / genus' and do not contradict any genus values.
